content.assessment: No directly comparable international case of capacity-capped contraction (holding admission criteria fixed) is supplied. The closest analogues concern structural delay reforms (Finland, Poland) rather than capacity-threshold manipulation within an unchanged selective structure. However, the Hungary-specific finding that tracking's harm operates through concentrating between-school variance (F-live-international_comparison-02) suggests contraction, by shrinking the selective pool, could plausibly reduce the scale of between-school sorting even without changing selection criteria—though this is an inference, not a tested comparative mechanism. The proposal's own risk (church/private absorption) mirrors the general comparative lesson that Hungary's segregation is a system-wide, multi-maintainer phenomenon (F-live-international_comparison-06), and a state-sector-only lever addresses only part of that system.
opportunities: Could be paired with insights from Hungary's own tracking-choice interaction finding to more precisely target which localities' contraction most reduces segregation (F-live-international_comparison-09)
strengths: Consistent with the diagnosis that between-school variance (not within-school) is where Hungarian inequality concentrates (F-live-international_comparison-02), so shrinking the between-school-sorting pool has a plausible theoretical basis
threats: Because church/private schools sit outside state jurisdiction and Hungary's growth in selective church-school share is a documented pattern, contraction of the state sector alone risks merely reallocating rather than reducing the segregation mechanism identified as structurally central (F-live-international_comparison-01, F-live-international_comparison-06)
weaknesses: No comparable international mechanism (capacity-threshold contraction alone) is present in the evidence base to assess transferability or outcome relevance
Does not address the ethnic-segregation dimension specific to Hungary (F-live-international_comparison-06), which has no parallel in the Finnish/German/Austrian tracking debates cited
